title: Bank Reconciliation Lab
subhead: The bank says one number. Your books say another. Reconciliation is how you find out why — and it's the single most common technical interview question in entry-level accounting.
hook: "Walk me through how you'd handle a bank reconciliation discrepancy." You will be asked this. After this module, you'll have a real answer with a real dollar amount attached to it.

chunks.title: Why the bank balance and the book balance almost never match on day one
chunks.bodyHtml:
At any given moment, your books and the bank's records are usually a little out of sync — not because something is wrong, but because of timing:

  • Deposits in transit — you recorded a customer payment, but it hasn't hit the bank yet.
  • Outstanding checks — you wrote a check and recorded it, but the vendor hasn't cashed it yet.
  • Bank fees / interest — the bank deducts or adds something you haven't recorded yet.

Reconciliation is the process of proving that, once you account for timing differences, the adjusted bank balance and the adjusted book balance are exactly equal. If they're not equal after adjusting for timing, something is actually wrong — a duplicate, a typo, a missing transaction — and you have to find it.

chunks.title: The two-sided reconciliation
chunks.bodyHtml:
Every reconciliation works from both directions and they have to meet in the middle:

From the bank sideFrom the book side
Ending balance per bank statementEnding balance per your ledger
+ Deposits in transit+ Bank credits not yet recorded (interest, etc.)
− Outstanding checks− Bank debits not yet recorded (fees, etc.)
= Adjusted bank balance= Adjusted book balance

These two numbers should be identical. If they're not, the difference IS the error — and finding it is the actual job.

callout.html: Real example from January's close at Skarion: the bank statement showed $12,450 and QuickBooks showed $12,180 — a $270 gap. The cause: a Valley Freight payment was miscoded. Finding gaps like this, and explaining exactly how you traced them, is what interviewers are listening for.

chunks.title: Common reconciliation problems — know them cold

cards.back: A customer payment gets recorded in the books twice. January's real example: a $6,800 deposit (ref DEP-1005) was entered twice — corrected with Debit Accounts Receivable / Credit Cash to reinstate the receivable.
A check was written and recorded in the books, but the vendor hasn't cashed it yet — it won't show on the bank statement until they do.
The book entry doesn't match what actually cleared. Example: rent was recorded at $12,750 but the bank shows $12,800 actually cleared — a $50 correction is needed.
The bank charges a monthly service fee or similar that never got entered into the books — shows as an unexplained gap until you add it.
You made a deposit right at month-end that hasn't posted to the bank statement yet — it'll show up next month.

questions.q: Bank statement shows $12,450. QuickBooks shows $12,180. There's a $270 gap. What's the right first move?
options: Change the QuickBooks balance to match the bank — problem solved
Trace the gap to its source transaction before adjusting anything
Ignore it — a $270 difference is too small to matter
Call the bank and ask them to fix their records
questions.explanation: Never force numbers to match. Always trace the specific transaction causing the gap first — in this case a miscoded Valley Freight payment — then make the correcting entry.

questions.q: A $6,800 customer deposit was accidentally entered into the books twice. What's the correcting journal entry?
options: No entry needed — it will fix itself next month
questions.explanation: The duplicate entry incorrectly cleared the customer's invoice twice — revenue was never overstated. The fix reinstates the receivable: Debit Accounts Receivable, Credit Cash.

questions.q: You wrote check #1018 for $4,925 and recorded it in the books on January 28th. The vendor hasn't cashed it by January 31st. How does this affect the reconciliation?
options: It's an outstanding check — subtract it from the bank side to get the adjusted bank balance
It's a deposit in transit — add it to the bank side
It's an error and must be reversed
It has no effect on the reconciliation
questions.explanation: It's in your books but hasn't cleared the bank yet — a classic outstanding check. Subtract it from the bank statement balance when calculating the adjusted bank balance.

chunks.title: Correct the duplicate deposit
chunks.scenarioHtml: January's bank reconciliation surfaced a real problem: customer ACH deposit ref DEP-1005 for $6,800.00 was recorded in QuickBooks twice — once as the real payment, once as a duplicate entry. The duplicate incorrectly cleared the customer's invoice a second time. Post the correcting entry to reinstate the receivable.

chunks.title: Reconcile all three months
chunks.instructionsHtml:
Download all three bank statement workbooks below. Each has an Instructions tab, the Bank Statement, the QuickBooks Register, and a Reconciliation Worksheet. For each month:

  1. Match every transaction between the bank statement and the register.
  2. Identify the 2-3 seeded problems (duplicates, wrong amounts, outstanding checks, unrecorded fees).
  3. Complete the Reconciliation Worksheet until the difference is exactly $0.00.

Watch the labels carefully — the March worksheet has a row marked (+/-) because that month's duplicate is a duplicated debit, not a duplicated credit like January's.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: Write your reconciliation memo for one month: what was the discrepancy, how did you find it, and how did you fix it?
